speaker: interviewer
text: Today's March 31, 1997. The interviewer is Shulamit Bastacky. And I'm conducting interview with a ex-sergeant of US Army, William McKinney. Interview's conducted in English in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Would you please tell me your first and your last name and spelling, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: William N. McKinney. And you want me to spell it? W-I-L-L-I-A-M, N as in Nathaniel, McKinney, M-C-K-I-N-N-E-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any-- or do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that spelling of that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-I-L-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 1, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old does it make you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and state were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the state of Pennsylvania, in Uniontown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give us the spelling of the city where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: U-I-O-N-T-O-W-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that state of Pennsylvania? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the state of Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: In country of USA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Country, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to us your family? Tell us a little bit about your early childhood? Where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. And I come from a family of six boys and four girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did your parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- my mother was a housekeeper in our home. And my father, he worked various jobs. He was-- he served in World War I. And he was gassed. And he worked various jobs to try to raise the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he was gassed. Can you explain that more? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he fought in the trenches in France with the 377th Infantry Division. And during World War I, they were using different types of gas, mainly mustard gas. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a consequence, he was gassed in those trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: He survived this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived. And he lived to be 94.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect his life, experiencing this in-- in-- in the-- in the First War? What was-- did it affect his health?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said he was gassed. And he-- when he died, he died with some sort of kidney failure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the community where you lived in Uniontown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was brought away from Uniontown when I was 10 months old, so it'd be very difficult for me to describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you grew up in Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to us a little bit your life with your other siblings, your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was compatible. We had a home that was full of love. And we were all hard workers. And we had time for play-- play and work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in your-- among your siblings, of the 10 brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was next to the baby boy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I had three other brothers older than I. And can you describe your-- your childhood, what kind of things you did when you were growing boy in Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we'd go out in the woods. We lived in Penn Township. Penn Hills, today they call it. My baby brother and I'd go out in the woods and pick elderberries, apples, and so forth. My mother would make elderberry jelly, and apple jelly, and apple sauce, and so forth. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house where you lived with your family? Was it a big house, small house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a large house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how many-- how many rooms? How big of a house was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: About eight rooms. The area where you lived, was it-- what kind of a neighborhood was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a mixed neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mixed? Mixed in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was both black and white families. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we all got along. And what kind of holidays did you celebrate together [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the normal Christian holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which--

speaker: interviewee
text: Christmas, Easter, Fourth of July-- Independence Day-- Memorial Day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what kind of high school did you go to? Which government school you went to? Was it racially mixed? What-- what was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was racially mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated in-- in-- in high school? What year was it when you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it would've been 1939, about '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us a little bit of your-- your high school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went to high school, I wasn't no A student, but just a mediocre student. And the thing that fascinated me most was the sciences, biology and-- and physics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed those studies the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take part in any activities after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my baby brother and I, we would play together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not when I was a youngster, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what happened after you finished high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went off to the military. That was in 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went to the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18, turning 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us what was it when you graduated from high school and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you were-- were you drafted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this thing for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was drafted. My family had moved from Penn Township to Stanley Heights district, off of-- on Centre Avenue. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was drafted from [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Draft Board number five at Herron Hill Junior High School. And that's where I was drafted into the military, June 6, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were drafted, when you were still in high school, did you experience [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] racism in school or in your area where you lived? Can you describe that for us a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't experience anything out of the ordinary. It was racially mixed and balanced. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Racial mix and what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Racially mixed and balanced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Balanced.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did not-- you did not have any major problem related to race in high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you go now with us chronologically what was it like on the day that you were drafted, that you were taken to the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the day that I was drafted, there was many young men down at the draft board downtown at the old post office building in downtown Pittsburgh. At the old post office building. And there were-- oh, about-- a little over 300 men there, young men. And they're-- oh, they're ranging in age from 18 to 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us various tests and so forth. And one test in particular that they gave was a communication. And I scored the highest out of all the men. And it was Morse code. And then they put me in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] air base security battalion--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to transmit-- that was at Camp Rucker, Alabama.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were drafted, can you describe to us what was the day like when you changed from the civilian to the military man and the-- said-- you said goodbye to your families. What-- can you describe that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I said goodbye to my mother and dad, and my-- there was two other brothers and myself. We went to downtown to the B and O station to ship out for various designations. And my fiancee, she was there, along with her sister. She was a twin. And she [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] saw me off. And I told her-- I said, I will get married, but it wouldn't be fair to my mother, and because a-- on induction, they got-- each soldier was insured for $10,000. And I said, if anything happened to me, we would have not been married long enough for your entitlement to this $10,000. And I said, my mother has done more for me up to this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I named my mother as the beneficiary if anything should happen to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, as I promised her, after the war, we got married. And from the marriage, we had three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go a little bit earlier, before we go to the period after the war. Did you go through basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that for us a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I arrived at Fort Meade, Maryland. And I got the necessary inoculation shots. And then from Fort Meade, I was shipped to Camp Rucker, Alabama. And my other brothers, one was sent to-- Fort Belvoir, Virginia, do and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my baby brother was sent to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a camp in Michigan. I don't remember just what the name of the camp was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the-- the basic training for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first, the outfit that I was in, the lowest rank was corporal technician. They had all technical corporal sergeants, and master sergeant, and first sergeant. And I was put under-- they-- they gave us a test again. And they had narrowed it down to 76 men. And out of the 76, I scored the highest again on communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Morse code again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morse code. Then they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] put me under the direct [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] learning techniques of a Sergeant Younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a direct learning technique?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's more or less he was going to teach me the ins and outs of Morse code. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I started it-- well, the bigger part of my days were spent with him, learning the ins and outs of Morse code.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this part of the-- of the basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was part of my basic training. Then they had these air base security battalions in North Africa. And two or three of them, they got wiped completely out. And see, air base security battalion was similar to the infantry. They were supposed to guard the airfields. And so they broke the air base security battalion up and transferred us all into the quartermaster. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's-- from there, they-- I was only at Camp Rucker about a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Alabama?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alabama. Then they sent us to Camp Shelby, Mississippi. And it was a hellhole. That's where I began to experience a great deal of prejudice, and indifference, and so forth, and name-calling and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it for us? Give us examples?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they hauled us in trucks, when the trucks pulled up on the base, I saw these beautiful white barracks. And I saw in-- you know, manicured lawns. I thought, this is a nice camp. They drove by the barracks and took us down to the back end of the camp. And they had these little 4-by-4 black huts. No windows. They had [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] panels that they would raise and lower. You know, it-- it was screened. When it was hot, you just raised it during the day. And if it was a cool night, you'd lower it. And I think they slept six to a barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we encountered some difficulties with the white civilians and the white soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what kind of difficulties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was name-calling. And-- and then there were fights that broke out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us an example of how-- what kind of words they use towards the black soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would call us niggers and blackie and all sorts of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Black what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blackies. Nigger. And all sorts of name-calling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: And spit at us and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these the white fellow soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: White soldiers. See, the soldiers weren't integrated then. They had all-black outfits and a white outfit. And so we have a colonel. His name was Colonel Castle. He was a battalion commander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he white or black?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a white man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he treated us-- he was a good man. And so-- because Colonel Castle, he was the one that was responsible to have our outfit transferred out of Camp Shelby, Mississippi, because he told Washington, DC-- the high command in Washington-- told them if they didn't ship us out of there, he would not be held responsible for what would happen. He was going to arm the battalion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Army battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was going to arm us-- arm the battalion and let them go up against these white soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was only there three weeks. Then they shipped us to Camp Forrest, Tennessee. That's where I completed my basic training, at Camp Forrest, Tennessee. And how was the treatment to you as being black American there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Camp Forrest? It was somewhat similar, but it wasn't as bad as it was in Mississippi. The-- you encountered a lot of prejudice in the South-- well, at Tennessee, Alabama, Georgia. All the states where they had these camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have time--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were segregated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a time to go out of the base and socialize with the local population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You get passes and go into town. And at Camp Forrest, you had [INAUDIBLE] you-- you could go to, or Memphis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what it was like for you to go on-- on the-- on the leave out of your base? How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, generally as a rule, we would find black communities. And we were treated well. And a lot of places I had read about when I was in school-- when I'd go on pass, my chief interest was to see these places and visit these places, like Meharry. It was a black university there in Tennessee, in Nashville. And I'd go out there, meet with different persons. And then there was a Jackson [INAUDIBLE]. I'd go there and have lunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you free to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And stayed at the YMCA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you free to go to any type of restaurants, or was it also segregated down south?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, in the white community, they had their restaurants. And in the black community, they had their restaurants.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you able to go to a white restaurant in South?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Most of the restaurants that I frequented when I went into town were black.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we are coming to the end of this tape. And we will continue on the next tape.
